# Dune CMS — llms-full.txt

This file contains comprehensive agent-oriented documentation for Dune CMS, including all skill files. For the concise version, see https://dune.dev/llms.txt

---

# PART 1: Quick Reference

Dune is a Deno-native, flat-file CMS with TypeScript throughout. Content lives in Markdown/MDX/TSX files. The core primitive is the plugin. The data layer uses a unified YAML schema format. Auth is built-in and sessionless-by-default.

Full docs: https://dune.dev/docs

---

## Project layout

```
my-site/
  site.yaml               # all top-level config
  content/                # Markdown, MDX, TSX pages
    01.home/
      default.md          # → route /
    02.blog/
      default.md          # → route /blog
      01.hello-world.md   # → route /blog/hello-world
    _drafts/              # non-routable (underscore prefix)
  schemas/                # data model definitions (YAML)
    comments.yaml
    subscriptions.yaml
  plugins/                # project plugins
    comments.ts
    newsletter.ts
  jobs/                   # background jobs (auto-registered)
    weekly-digest.ts
  emails/                 # email templates
    welcome.email.tsx
    digest.email.mdx
  themes/                 # rendering layer
    default/
      templates/
        default.tsx
        post.tsx
      static/
        style.css
  data/                   # runtime data (flat files)
    users/
  .dune/                  # runtime scratch (gitignore this)
```

Numeric prefixes on folders/files control sort order and are stripped from routes. `default.md` inside a folder is that folder's index page. Underscore-prefixed folders are non-routable.

---

## site.yaml top-level keys

```yaml
site:
  name: My Site
  url: https://example.com
  homepage: 01.home

auth:
  mode: dune              # or: external-jwt
  userStore: local        # session | local | db
  providers:
    github: { clientId: "...", clientSecret: "..." }
    magicLink: { enabled: true }

content:
  taxonomies: [tags, category]

i18n:
  defaultLanguage: en
  languages: [en, de, fr]

email:
  from: "My Site <hello@example.com>"
  provider: resend        # smtp | postmark | sendgrid
  resend: { apiKey: "${RESEND_API_KEY}" }

db:
  url: "${DATABASE_URL}"

system:
  debug: false
  port: 8000
```

---

## CLI reference

```sh
dune dev              # start dev server (watch mode)
dune build            # production build
dune build --static   # generate static site
dune validate         # whole-project lint
dune codegen          # regenerate types from schemas
dune jobs:run <name>  # run a job immediately
dune deploy:init fly  # scaffold fly.toml for Fly.io
dune update:skills    # reinstall skills from current package version
```

---

## Common tasks

| Task | Where to look |
|------|---------------|
| Add login/logout | Auth section below / dune-auth skill |
| Gate content by role | Add `roles:` to frontmatter |
| Check permission in code | `authz.check()` — dune-authz skill |
| Define a data model | schemas/*.yaml — dune-schemas skill |
| Write a plugin | plugins/*.ts — dune-plugin-authoring skill |
| Schedule a recurring task | jobs/*.ts — dune-jobs skill |
| Send email | `email.send()` — dune-email skill |
| Create content | content/**/*.{md,mdx,tsx} — dune-content skill |

---

# PART 2: Skill Files

The following sections are the complete Dune skill files installed into `.claude/skills/` by `dune new`. Each covers one domain with patterns, working examples, and gotchas.

---

# Skill: Dune Auth

Public (non-admin) user authentication. Two modes — pick one in `site.yaml`. Both produce the same `ctx.state.user` shape; all middleware works identically regardless of mode.

---

## Mode 1: `dune` — built-in session stack

Dune owns the session lifecycle. Credential verification is delegated to OAuth providers or magic link. **No passwords.**

### Minimal config (OAuth only, no server-side user store)

```yaml
# site.yaml
auth:
  mode: dune
  userStore: session       # OAuth claims encoded into cookie — no file or DB needed
  session:
    secret: "${SESSION_SECRET}"
    ttl: 7d
  providers:
    github:
      clientId: "${GITHUB_CLIENT_ID}"
      clientSecret: "${GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET}"
```

### With persistent user records + magic link (no DB required)

```yaml
auth:
  mode: dune
  userStore: local          # flat files at data/users/ + Deno KV index
  providers:
    github:
      clientId: "${GITHUB_CLIENT_ID}"
      clientSecret: "${GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET}"
    magicLink:
      enabled: true         # passwordless — requires email config
```

### With pre-created local users (no OAuth)

```yaml
auth:
  mode: dune
  userStore: local
  providers:
    localUsers:
      enabled: true         # users pre-created by admin in data/users/
    magicLink:
      enabled: true
```

`localUsers` is not a registration flow. Users are created by the admin (via `/admin/users/` or by editing files). Self-registration goes through an OAuth provider.

### Routes generated by mode 1

```
GET  /auth/login               → provider selection (or direct redirect if one provider)
GET  /auth/github              → begin OAuth flow
GET  /auth/github/callback     → complete OAuth, create session, redirect
GET  /auth/magic               → accept token from email link, create session
POST /auth/magic/send          → generate + send magic link email
POST /auth/logout              → destroy session
```

---

## Mode 2: `external-jwt` — validate and trust

Dune validates a JWT on each request, populates `ctx.state.user` from the claims, and does nothing else. No sessions, no OAuth flow, no user storage. The external service (Clerk, Auth0, Supabase Auth) owns everything.

```yaml
# site.yaml — Clerk example
auth:
  mode: external-jwt
  jwt:
    issuer: "https://your-app.clerk.accounts.dev"
    audience: "your-app"
    jwksUrl: "https://your-app.clerk.accounts.dev/.well-known/jwks.json"
    userClaims:             # map JWT claims → ctx.state.user fields
      id: sub
      email: email
      name: name
```

No routes generated. No sessions. No user records in Dune.

---

## `ctx.state.user` shape

Both modes populate the same shape. Always check for null before accessing fields.

```ts
interface PublicUser {
  id: string;
  email: string;
  name?: string;
  avatarUrl?: string;
  provider: string;       // "github" | "google" | "magic-link" | "clerk" | ...
  roles?: string[];       // group memberships — populated from JWT claims (external-jwt)
                          // or from authz tuples (local/db userStore)
  createdAt?: Date;       // absent in external-jwt mode
}

// ctx.state.user is null on unauthenticated requests — never undefined
```

---

## Protecting routes

### Redirect unauthenticated users

```ts
// routes/_middleware.ts  (or any nested _middleware.ts)
import type { FreshContext } from "fresh";

export async function handler(req: Request, ctx: FreshContext) {
  if (!ctx.state.user) {
    return Response.redirect(new URL("/auth/login", req.url));
  }
  return ctx.next();
}
```

`ctx.state.user` is populated globally on every request — no opt-in middleware required to get the value. The middleware above only adds the redirect gate.

### Require a specific role

```ts
import { authz } from "@/auth/authz.ts";

export async function handler(req: Request, ctx: FreshContext) {
  if (!ctx.state.user) {
    return Response.redirect(new URL("/auth/login", req.url));
  }
  const allowed = await authz.check({
    who: { type: "user", id: ctx.state.user.id },
    canThey: "access",
    onWhat: { type: "group", id: "member" },
  });
  if (!allowed) return new Response(null, { status: 403 });
  return ctx.next();
}
```

For content gating via frontmatter `roles:`, this check runs automatically in the page middleware — no manual wiring needed. See **dune-authz** skill.

---

## `userStore` tiers (mode: dune only)

| Value | User records | When to use |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| `session` | None — OAuth claims encoded in cookie | OAuth-only, no need to identify users across requests beyond the session |
| `local` | `data/users/*.json` + Deno KV index | Default. No DB required. Supports localUsers, magicLink, OAuth. |
| `db` | DB table | Opt-in. Requires `db-schema-layer`. Use at scale or when admin UI user management is needed. |

`userStore: session` does not support `localUsers` — there's nowhere to store pre-created users. It only works with OAuth providers.

---

## Gotchas

**No passwords.** Password auth is explicitly out of scope. Magic link covers the passwordless use case. If a user needs to log in without OAuth, use `localUsers` + `magicLink`.

**`localUsers` always implies `userStore: local`.** You cannot use `localUsers` with `userStore: session` — users must exist somewhere before they can authenticate.

**`ctx.state.user` is `null`, not `undefined`.** Always check `if (!ctx.state.user)`, not `if (ctx.state.user === undefined)`.

**`external-jwt` eliminates user storage entirely.** Dune doesn't own user records, roles, or sessions in this mode. `ctx.state.user.roles` comes from JWT claims, not from `authz` tuples. Do not call `authz.addMember()` or `authz.allow()` expecting them to affect JWT claims.

**Session secret must be set.** `SESSION_SECRET` must be a strong random value (32+ bytes). Omitting it or using a weak value is a security issue. Generate with: `openssl rand -base64 32`.

**Magic link rate limiting.** `POST /auth/magic/send` has a fixed-window rate limit owned by Dune. If using an external provider (Clerk etc.) for magic links, rate limiting is that provider's concern.

---

# Skill: Dune Authorization (Polizy)

Authorization in Dune uses [polizy](https://github.com/bratsos/polizy) — a Zanzibar-inspired relationship-based model. One `authz.check()` call covers everything: admin panel access, inline editing, content gating, route middleware, and resource-level permissions.

For polizy's core concepts (`defineSchema()`, tuple model, check algorithm, `listAccessibleObjects`), see the polizy skills installed alongside this one. This skill covers Dune-specific wiring and patterns.

---

## The single authz file

`src/auth/authz.ts` is scaffolded by `dune add polizy`. Do not create it manually. It has two sections:

```ts
// src/auth/authz.ts

import { AuthSystem, defineSchema } from "polizy";
import { AuthzLocalAdapter } from "@dune/core";  // or AuthzDbAdapter

// Section 1: schema — store-agnostic, defines relations/actions/hierarchy
const schema = defineSchema({
  // ... see polizy skill for defineSchema() syntax
  // Dune's default schema covers: admin, editor, author, member, owner
  // Actions: access, pages.update, users.manage, media.upload, edit, ...
});

// Section 2: AuthSystem — wires the adapter from authzStore config
export const authz = new AuthSystem({
  schema,
  storage: new AuthzLocalAdapter(),  // swapped to AuthzDbAdapter when authzStore: db
});
```

Import `authz` from this file everywhere permission checks are needed:

```ts
import { authz } from "@/auth/authz.ts";
```

---

## Config

```yaml
# site.yaml
auth:
  mode: dune
  authzStore: local    # default — data/permissions/*.json + Deno KV index
  # authzStore: db     # opt-in — requires db-schema-layer
```

`authzStore` is independent of `userStore`. You can have `userStore: session` and `authzStore: local`.

---

## Common check patterns

All checks are async. Always `await`.

### Admin panel access

```ts
const canAdmin = await authz.check({
  who: { type: "user", id: ctx.state.user.id },
  canThey: "access",
  onWhat: { type: "app", id: "admin" },
});
```

### Group membership (content gating, membership sites)

```ts
const isMember = await authz.check({
  who: { type: "user", id: ctx.state.user.id },
  canThey: "access",
  onWhat: { type: "group", id: "member" },
});
```

### Inline editing / resource-level permission

```ts
const canEdit = await authz.check({
  who: { type: "user", id: ctx.state.user.id },
  canThey: "edit",
  onWhat: { type: "resource", id: pageRoute },
});
```

### Specific admin action

```ts
const canManageUsers = await authz.check({
  who: { type: "user", id: ctx.state.user.id },
  canThey: "users.manage",
  onWhat: { type: "app", id: "admin" },
});
```

---

## Granting permissions

### Add user to a group

```ts
await authz.addMember({
  member: { type: "user", id: userId },
  group: { type: "group", id: "member" },
});
```

Call this on successful OAuth login or payment to grant group access.

### Grant a direct resource permission

```ts
await authz.allow({
  who: { type: "user", id: userId },
  canThey: "edit",
  onWhat: { type: "resource", id: pageRoute },
});
```

Use for per-resource grants (e.g., an OAuth user who is the owner of a specific page).

---

## Content gating via frontmatter

For pages with `roles:` frontmatter, the check runs automatically in Dune's page middleware — **do not wire this manually**.

```yaml
# content/members/welcome.md
---
roles: member
---
```

```yaml
roles: [member, admin]        # OR — any of these is sufficient
roles:
  all: [member, verified]     # AND — user must belong to every group
```

Unauthenticated users are redirected to `/auth/login`. Authenticated users without the required role get a 403.

---

## Route middleware pattern

For programmatic route protection (not frontmatter-based):

```ts
// routes/dashboard/_middleware.ts
import { FreshContext } from "fresh";
import { authz } from "@/auth/authz.ts";

export async function handler(req: Request, ctx: FreshContext) {
  if (!ctx.state.user) {
    return Response.redirect(new URL("/auth/login", req.url));
  }
  const allowed = await authz.check({
    who: { type: "user", id: ctx.state.user.id },
    canThey: "access",
    onWhat: { type: "group", id: "member" },
  });
  if (!allowed) return new Response(null, { status: 403 });
  return ctx.next();
}
```

---

## Bootstrap path (existing admin users)

On first startup after polizy is introduced, Dune derives initial tuples from the `role` field on existing user files (`admin | editor | author`). No manual migration step.

```json
// data/users/alice.json
{ "id": "alice", "email": "alice@example.com", "role": "editor" }
```

→ Dune creates tuples equivalent to `authz.allow({ who: user:alice, canThey: "access", onWhat: app:admin })` and the editor-level action grants on first run.

From then on, **tuples are the authority**. The `role` field becomes a legacy hint. Use `authz.allow()` and `authz.addMember()` to manage permissions going forward.

---

## Permission tuple storage

`authzStore: local` stores tuples as JSON files:

```
data/permissions/
  {id}.json   →  { id, subject, relation, object, condition? }
```

The Deno KV index covers hot lookup paths. If KV is lost, it rebuilds from files on startup (mtime comparison). Do not edit these files directly — use `authz.allow()`, `authz.addMember()`, `authz.revoke()` etc.

---

## Gotchas

**`authz.check()` is async — always await it.** It makes multiple storage calls. Dropping the `await` returns a Promise, not a boolean, and your condition will always be truthy.

**Do not manually check `ctx.state.user.roles` for group membership.** In `userStore: local` or `db` mode, `roles` reflects current group membership but going through `authz.check()` is the correct path — it handles hierarchy and inheritance. Direct `roles` array inspection bypasses that.

**Content-gated pages: do not add manual `authz.check()` in `_middleware.ts`.** The page middleware reads `roles:` frontmatter and calls `authz.check()` automatically. Doubling up results in two checks and potentially inconsistent behavior.

**`external-jwt` mode: authz tuples are irrelevant for roles.** In `external-jwt` mode, `ctx.state.user.roles` comes from JWT claims. `authz.addMember()` writes to the local/db tuple store, which is never consulted in this mode. Don't mix the two.

**`src/auth/authz.ts` schema is yours to maintain.** `dune add polizy` scaffolds it; after that it's application code. When you add a new relation or action, update `defineSchema()` here. The adapter doesn't need to change.

---

# Skill: Dune Plugin Authoring

Plugins extend Dune via hooks that fire at defined points in the content and request lifecycle. A plugin is a TypeScript module that exports a `DunePlugin` object. Agents frequently place plugin files in the wrong location or omit security guards on admin routes — both are addressed here.

---

## File location

```
plugins/
  my-plugin/
    mod.ts        ← entry point for multi-file plugins
    admin.ts      ← admin route handlers (optional, import from mod.ts)
    types.ts      ← shared types (optional)
  simple-plugin.ts  ← single-file plugins can live here directly
```

Dune does not scan `plugins/` automatically — plugins must be registered (see below). Do not place plugin files anywhere else in the project.

---

## Registration

```yaml
# site.yaml
plugins:
  - src: plugins/my-plugin/mod.ts      # local plugin
  - spec: jsr:@dune/blog@^1.2.0        # JSR plugin — pin the major version
  - spec: npm:dune-comments@^2.0.0     # npm plugin
```

Local plugins use `src:`. Remote plugins use `spec:` with a pinned version. Unpinned plugin specs fail `dune validate`.

---

## Minimal plugin shape

```ts
// plugins/my-plugin/mod.ts
import type { DunePlugin } from "@dune/core";

export default {
  name: "my-plugin",
  version: "1.0.0",
  hooks: {},
} satisfies DunePlugin;
```

`satisfies DunePlugin` catches shape errors at compile time. Always use it.

---

## Hook context

Every hook receives `ctx` as its first argument:

```ts
interface PluginContext {
  content: ContentAPI;     // query the content index
  email: EmailAPI;         // send transactional email
  db: DbAPI;               // query app data (requires db-schema-layer)
  config: SiteConfig;      // read site.yaml values
  logger: Logger;          // structured logging
}
```

---

## Common hook patterns

### React to content changes

```ts
hooks: {
  onContentLoad: async (ctx) => {
    const posts = await ctx.content.find({ type: "post" });
    ctx.logger.info("plugin.content_loaded", { postCount: posts.length });
  },
}
```

### Cascade delete when a page is removed

```ts
hooks: {
  onPageDelete: async (ctx, page) => {
    await ctx.db.comments.delete({ where: { pageRoute: page.route } });
    await ctx.db.reactions.delete({ where: { pageRoute: page.route } });
  },
}
```

### Send email on a content event

```ts
hooks: {
  onPagePublish: async (ctx, page) => {
    if (page.frontmatter.notifySubscribers) {
      await ctx.email.send({
        to: "list@example.com",
        subject: `New post: ${page.title}`,
        template: "new-post",
        data: { title: page.title, route: page.route },
      });
    }
  },
}
```

### Modify page data before render

```ts
hooks: {
  onPageRender: async (ctx, page) => {
    page.data.relatedPosts = await ctx.content.find({
      type: "post",
      taxonomy: { category: page.frontmatter.category },
      limit: 3,
    });
  },
}
```

---

## Admin routes

**All admin routes require security guards. Omitting them is a HIGH severity vulnerability.**

### GET route (read-only)

```ts
import type { DunePlugin, FreshContext } from "@dune/core";
import { requirePermission } from "@dune/core";

export default {
  name: "my-plugin",
  version: "1.0.0",
  hooks: {
    onAdminRoutes: (router) => {
      router.get(
        "/admin/my-plugin",
        requirePermission("pages.view"),   // ← required
        listHandler,
      );
    },
  },
} satisfies DunePlugin;
```

### POST route (mutation)

```ts
onAdminRoutes: (router) => {
  router.post(
    "/admin/api/my-plugin/action",
    requirePermission("pages.update"),   // ← required
    csrfCheck,                           // ← required on all mutations
    actionHandler,
  );
},
```

```ts
import { requirePermission, csrfCheck } from "@dune/core";
```

**Both `requirePermission` and `csrfCheck` are required on every mutation route.**

### Permission reference

| Action | Permission string |
|--------|-----------------|
| View any admin page | `"pages.view"` |
| Create / update pages | `"pages.update"` |
| Delete pages | `"pages.delete"` |
| Manage users | `"users.manage"` |
| Upload media | `"media.upload"` |
| Manage plugins | `"plugins.manage"` |

---

## Testing a plugin

```ts
import { createTestHarness } from "@dune/testing";
import myPlugin from "./plugins/my-plugin/mod.ts";

const harness = await createTestHarness({
  content: {
    "01.home/default.md": "---\ntitle: Home\n---\nHello",
  },
  plugins: [myPlugin],
});

const page = await harness.render("/home");
assertEquals(page.html.includes("Hello"), true);

await harness.dispose();
```

---

## Gotchas

**Wrong file location.** Plugin files must be in `plugins/`. Placing them in `src/`, `routes/`, or the project root means they won't be found by `dune validate` and the plugin spec won't resolve correctly.

**Forgetting `csrfCheck` on mutations.** Every `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE` admin route needs `csrfCheck`. GET routes do not. If in doubt, add it — it's a no-op on safe methods.

**`requirePermission` does not replace `csrfCheck`.** They guard different things. Use both on every mutation route.

**Hook context `db` requires db-schema-layer.** `ctx.db` is only available if `db-schema-layer` is configured. Check `ctx.config.db?.enabled` if your plugin needs to be db-optional.

**`onAdminRoutes` fires before the server starts.** Do not perform async operations inside `onAdminRoutes` itself — only register routes. Async work belongs in the route handlers.

**Hook errors do not crash the server.** An unhandled error in a hook is logged and skipped. Handle errors explicitly if your hook has required side effects.

**Pin JSR/npm plugin versions.** `spec: jsr:@dune/blog` without a version pinned fails validation. Use `^major.minor.patch` at minimum.

---

# Skill: Dune Schema Layer

All data models — whether file-backed or database-backed — are defined in `schemas/` using a unified YAML format. The `store:` field determines the runtime interface and backend. **`store:` is required; omitting it is an error.**

```
schemas/
  posts.yaml       # store: local — YAML files, slug-as-id, local.* interface
  comments.yaml    # store: db    — SQLite/KV/Postgres, UUID id, db.* interface
```

`flex-objects/` is the legacy directory (backwards-compatible, not for new models).

---

## Schema format

```yaml
# schemas/comments.yaml
model: Comment
store: db
table: comments     # optional — defaults to snake_case plural of model name

fields:
  pageRoute:
    type: string
    required: true
    maxLength: 1024
    index: true

  author:
    type: string
    required: true
    maxLength: 256

  body:
    type: text
    required: true

  status:
    type: string
    enum: [pending, approved, rejected]
    default: pending
    index: true

  createdAt:
    type: datetime
    default: now

  updatedAt:
    type: datetime
    default: now
    onUpdate: now
```

### Field types

| Type | Notes |
|------|-------|
| `string` | Short text, maxLength applies |
| `text` | Long text, no maxLength, textarea in admin UI |
| `integer` | Whole numbers |
| `number` | Floating point |
| `boolean` | true / false |
| `datetime` | ISO timestamp. `default: now`, `onUpdate: now` supported |
| `json` | Arbitrary JSON blob |

`id` is always auto-generated — never declare it in the schema.

---

## `store: local` — file-backed models

Records live as YAML files in `content/<type>/`. The id is the filename stem (slug). Interface is `local.*`:

```ts
import { local } from "@/db";

const posts = await local.posts.find({ where: { publishedAt: { isNull: false } } });
const post  = await local.posts.findOne({ where: { id: "hello-world" } });
const draft = await local.posts.create({ data: { title: "Draft", body: "..." } });
await local.posts.update({ where: { id: "hello-world" }, data: { title: "Updated" } });
await local.posts.delete({ where: { id: "old-post" } });
```

---

## `store: db` — database-backed models

Records live in the configured DB backend. The id is a UUID string. Interface is `db.*`:

```ts
import { db } from "@/db";

const comments = await db.comments.find({
  where: { pageRoute: "/blog/hello", status: "approved" },
  orderBy: { field: "createdAt", dir: "desc" },
  limit: 20,
  offset: 0,
});

const created = await db.comments.create({
  data: { pageRoute, author, body },
});

await db.comments.update({ where: { id }, data: { status: "approved" } });
await db.comments.delete({ where: { id } });

// upsert — use instead of find-then-create to avoid race conditions
await db.subscribers.upsert({
  where: { email },
  create: { email, name },
  update: { name },
});
```

### `where` clause operators

```ts
where: { status: "approved" }                          // equality
where: { createdAt: { gt: new Date("2026-01-01") } }  // comparison
where: { status: { in: ["pending", "approved"] } }    // in list
where: { email: { contains: "@example.com" } }        // string contains
where: { deletedAt: { isNull: true } }                 // null check
where: { OR: [{ status: "pending" }, { status: "approved" }] }  // explicit OR
```

Full operator list: `eq`, `ne`, `in`, `notIn`, `lt`, `lte`, `gt`, `gte`, `contains`, `startsWith`, `isNull`.

### Escape hatches (transactions, aggregate queries)

```ts
import { getAdapter } from "@/db";
import type { SqliteAdapter } from "@dune/core";

await (getAdapter() as SqliteAdapter).transaction(async (tx) => {
  await tx.execute("INSERT INTO orders ...", [...]);
  await tx.execute("INSERT INTO audit_log ...", [...]);
});
```

---

## CRUD API generation

```yaml
# schemas/comments.yaml
api:
  enabled: true
  auth: required
  methods: [get, list]   # omit for full CRUD
```

Full CRUD generates: `GET /api/comments`, `GET /api/comments/:id`, `POST /api/comments`, `PUT /api/comments/:id`, `DELETE /api/comments/:id`.

---

## Codegen and migrations

```sh
dune codegen          # generates TypeScript types + @/db alias
dune migrate:generate # generates SQL migration files
dune migrate          # applies pending migrations
```

Always import from `@/db`, never relative paths.

---

## Gotchas

**`store:` is required.** Omitting it in `schemas/` is an error.

**`store: local` id is a slug, not a UUID.** `local.posts.findOne({ where: { id: "hello-world" } })` — the id is the filename stem.

**`findOne` throws when more than one row matches.** Use `find` with `limit: 1` if you expect multiple rows.

**Use `upsert` instead of find-then-create.** A `find` followed by a conditional `create` is a TOCTOU race under concurrent requests.

**Always import from `@/db`, never relative paths.** If `@/db` isn't resolving, run `dune codegen`.

**No relations in the query interface.** Do two queries. For cascade operations, use a plugin hook or the `getAdapter().transaction()` escape hatch.

---

# Skill: Dune Background Jobs

Background jobs are cron-scheduled tasks defined as TypeScript files in `jobs/`. Dune scans `jobs/*.ts` on startup and auto-registers all files that export a `schedule` and a default handler. No registration step required.

---

## Job file format

```ts
// jobs/weekly-digest.ts
import type { JobContext } from "@dune/core";

export const schedule = "0 9 * * MON";   // cron expression — required

export default async function handler(ctx: JobContext) {
  const posts = await ctx.content.find({ type: "post", limit: 5 });
  await ctx.email.send({
    to: "subscribers@example.com",
    subject: "Weekly digest",
    template: "digest",
    data: { posts },
  });
}
```

The job name is the filename stem (`weekly-digest`). One job per file.

### Cron expression format

```
"0 9 * * MON"     every Monday at 09:00
"0 0 * * *"       every day at midnight
"*/15 * * * *"    every 15 minutes
"0 2 1 * *"       1st of every month at 02:00
```

Standard five-field cron: `minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week`.

---

## Job context

`JobContext` is the same surface as plugin hook context: `content`, `email`, `db`, `config`, `logger`.

---

## Admin panel

`/admin/jobs` lists all registered jobs with `lastRun`, `nextRun`, `status`, `lastError`, and a manual trigger button.

```sh
dune jobs:list              # list all registered jobs with status
dune jobs:run weekly-digest # trigger a job immediately
```

---

## Error handling

When a handler throws, Dune logs the error, records `status: errored` in Deno KV, and continues scheduling future runs — **no retry**.

Handle expected errors explicitly:

```ts
export default async function handler(ctx: JobContext) {
  try {
    await sendDigest(ctx);
  } catch (err) {
    ctx.logger.error("jobs.digest_failed", { error: String(err) });
  }
}
```

---

## Gotchas

**`schedule` is required.** A file in `jobs/` without an exported `schedule` constant is silently ignored.

**One job per file.** The job name comes from the filename stem.

**No timeout enforcement.** Add your own timeout logic for jobs that call external services.

**No retry on error.** The next scheduled run is the natural retry.

**Multi-process duplicates.** If `workers > 1`, every worker runs every job. Use a single worker process or the BullMQ escape hatch for jobs that must not duplicate.

---

# Skill: Dune Email

Transactional email through a single `email.send()` call. Dune owns the interface and template rendering; the delivery provider is swappable via config. In development, emails are never sent — they're written to disk and visible in the admin panel.

---

## Call surface

```ts
import { email } from "@dune/core";

await email.send({
  to: "user@example.com",
  subject: "Your magic link",
  template: "magic-link",      // resolves emails/magic-link.email.{tsx,mdx,md}
  data: { link, expiresIn: "15 minutes" },
});
```

`to` accepts a string or `string[]`. `template` is the base filename without the `.email.{ext}` suffix.

---

## Provider config

```yaml
# site.yaml
email:
  from: "My Site <hello@example.com>"
  provider: resend
  resend:
    apiKey: "${RESEND_API_KEY}"
```

No provider config needed in development — dev-mode interception is automatic.

---

## Template formats

### TSX (`.email.tsx`) — structural, fully typed

```tsx
// emails/magic-link.email.tsx
export type Data = { link: string; expiresIn: string };

export default ({ link, expiresIn }: Data) => (
  <p>
    Click <a href={link}>here</a> to log in. Link expires in {expiresIn}.
  </p>
);
```

`export type Data` types the `data:` field — TypeScript catches mismatches at the call site.

### MDX (`.email.mdx`) — prose-heavy

```mdx
Hi {name},

Here are this week's top posts:

{posts.map(p => `- [${p.title}](${siteUrl}${p.route})`).join('\n')}
```

### Markdown (`.email.md`) — simplest

```md
Hi {{name}},

Click [here]({{link}}) to log in.
```

Dune does `{{key}}` substitution from `data:`, then renders Markdown to HTML.

---

## Dev-mode interception

In development, `email.send()` writes to `.dune/dev-email/` instead of sending. View at `/admin/email-preview`. **Clears on restart.**

---

## Common patterns

```ts
// Welcome email on first OAuth login (in a plugin hook):
hooks: {
  onUserCreate: async (ctx, user) => {
    await ctx.email.send({
      to: user.email,
      subject: `Welcome to ${ctx.config.site.name}`,
      template: "welcome",
      data: { name: user.name ?? user.email },
    });
  },
}
```

---

## Gotchas

**`template` is the base name only.** `template: "magic-link"` — not `"magic-link.email.tsx"`, not `"emails/magic-link"`.

**`export type Data` is only meaningful in TSX templates.** MDX and MD have no type-checked `data:`.

**MD uses `{{key}}`, not `{key}`.** `{link}` renders literally; `{{link}}` is substituted.

**Dev mode never sends.** `email.send()` in development always writes to disk regardless of provider config.

---

# Skill: Dune Content

Content conventions — file layout, naming, frontmatter, formats, templates, querying, taxonomy, and language variants.

---

## File layout and naming

```
content/
  01.home/
    default.md          → route: /
  02.blog/
    default.md          → route: /blog
    01.hello-world.md   → route: /blog/hello-world
  _components/          → non-routable (underscore prefix)
  _drafts/              → non-routable
```

| Convention | Effect |
|-----------|--------|
| `01.` numeric prefix | Stripped from route; controls sort order |
| `default.md` inside folder | That folder's index page |
| `_name/` underscore prefix | Non-routable module folder |
| `draft: true` in frontmatter | Excluded from index in production; visible in dev |

---

## Frontmatter reference

```yaml
---
title: My Post
description: SEO meta description
template: post                    # theme template — defaults to "default"
publishedAt: 2026-05-13
draft: false
tags: [deno, cms, tutorial]
category: technical
roles: member                     # content gating — see dune-authz skill
---
```

### `roles:` syntax

```yaml
roles: member                     # single group (OR)
roles: [member, admin]            # any of these (OR)
roles:
  all: [member, verified]         # all of these (AND)
```

Content gating is checked automatically — do not add manual middleware for content pages.

---

## Content formats

### Markdown (`.md`) — safe for untrusted authors, HTML sanitized by default

### MDX (`.mdx`) — Markdown with JSX component imports

```mdx
---
title: Interactive Post
---

import { Chart } from "./Chart.tsx"

## Results

<Chart data={frontmatter.data} />
```

Co-located imports are confined to the page's directory.

### TSX content pages (`.tsx`) — full Deno permissions, trusted authors only

```tsx
// content/blog/dashboard.tsx
import type { ContentPageProps } from "@dune/content/types";
import { db } from "@/db";

export default async function Dashboard({ route, page }: ContentPageProps) {
  const comments = await db.comments.find({ where: { pageRoute: route } });
  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{page.title}</h1>
      {comments.map(c => <div key={c.id}><p>{c.body}</p></div>)}
    </article>
  );
}
```

---

## Theme templates

Templates live in `themes/<name>/templates/*.tsx`:

```tsx
// themes/default/templates/post.tsx
import type { TemplateProps } from "@dune/content/types";

export default async function PostTemplate({ page, content, site }: TemplateProps) {
  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{page.title}</h1>
      <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: content }} />
    </article>
  );
}
```

| Prop | Type | Contents |
|------|------|----------|
| `page` | `PageMeta` | Frontmatter, route, template name, language |
| `content` | `string` | Rendered HTML (from md/mdx source) |
| `site` | `SiteConfig` | Values from `site.yaml` |

---

## Querying content

```ts
const posts = await ctx.content.find({
  type: "blog",                    // folder name with prefix stripped
  where: { draft: false },
  orderBy: "publishedAt",
  limit: 10,
});

const techPosts = await ctx.content.find({
  type: "blog",
  taxonomy: { category: "technical" },
});

const page = await ctx.content.findOne({ route: "/blog/hello-world" });
```

`type` maps to the folder name with the numeric prefix stripped.

---

## Taxonomy

```yaml
# site.yaml
content:
  taxonomies:
    - tags
    - category
```

```ts
const allCategories = await ctx.content.getTaxonomyValues("category");
const tagged = await ctx.content.find({ taxonomy: { tags: "deno" } });
```

---

## Language variants

```
content/02.blog/
  hello-world.md         → /blog/hello-world  (default language)
  hello-world.de.md      → /de/blog/hello-world
  hello-world.fr.md      → /fr/blog/hello-world
```

Language must be listed in `site.yaml` under `i18n.languages` to be detected.

---

## Gotchas

**Numeric prefix is stripped from routes.** Never construct URLs using the prefix.

**`default.md` is the folder index.** `index.md` is not special — it's a separate page.

**Underscore folders are non-routable.** Do not put content that should be publicly accessible in a folder with an underscore prefix.

**`type` in content queries is the stripped folder name.** Posts in `02.blog/` are `type: "blog"`.

**`template:` in frontmatter must match an actual file in the active theme.** `dune validate` catches this.

**TSX content pages require trusted authors.** They run with full Deno permissions — no sandbox.

**Language code must be configured to be detected.** Without it in `site.yaml`, the code is treated as part of the slug.

**`draft: true` behaves differently in dev vs production.** Don't rely on draft status as a security gate — use `roles:` frontmatter for access control.
